title: The Beginning and The End: `head` and `tail`
description: Extract the first or last lines of a file. Essential for log analysis.

slides.title: Just a Taste: `head` 👆
slides.content:
# First 10 Lines

`head` does exactly what it sounds like. It prints the **first** part of a file.

By default, it shows the **first 10 lines**.

```bash
head /var/log/syslog
```

### Why is this useful?
Log files can be thousands of lines. Sometimes you just need to see the most recent entries (which are at the **bottom**) or the file header. `head` gives you a clean snapshot of the top.

slides.title: See `head` in Action
slides.content: Generate a numbered list and view the first few lines.

slides.title: Controlling the Output: `-n` 🎛️
slides.content:
# Specify Line Count

10 lines not enough? Too many? Use the `-n` option to specify exactly how many lines you want.

```bash
head -n 5 filename.txt
```

Or the shorthand (no `-n`):
```bash
head -5 filename.txt
```

Both give you the first 5 lines.

quizData.question: To display the first 20 lines of `access.log`, the command is `head -_____ 20 access.log`.
quizData.explanation: The `-n` flag stands for 'number of lines'.

slides.title: The Trailing Edge: `tail` 👇
slides.content:
# Last 10 Lines

`tail` is the opposite of `head`. It prints the **last 10 lines** of a file.

```bash
tail /var/log/syslog
```

This is **THE MOST IMPORTANT COMMAND** for log analysis. Most logs append new events to the bottom. `tail` lets you see what just happened.

slides.title: Try `tail`
slides.content: View the last 5 lines of the numbers file we created.

slides.title: Live Monitoring: `tail -f` 👁️
slides.content:
# Follow Mode (Real-Time Hacking)

This is where the magic happens. The **`-f`** flag stands for **follow**.

```bash
tail -f /var/log/apache2/access.log
```

Instead of exiting after printing the last 10 lines, `tail -f` stays open. It watches the file and prints **new lines as they are written**.

### Scenario: The Hacker's Watchtower 🛡️
1. You suspect someone is brute-forcing your SSH server.
2. You run `tail -f /var/log/auth.log`.
3. You see login attempts scrolling live.
4. You block the IP instantly.

**To exit `tail -f`:** Press `Ctrl + C`.

quizData.question: You want to see new SSH attacks as they happen. Which command do you use?
quizData.explanation: `-f` continuously monitors the file for new additions in real-time.

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content:
## Negative Line Numbers (Advanced)

You can use negative numbers to exclude lines from the end.

`head -n -5 file.txt` -> Print everything **except** the last 5 lines.

`tail -n +20 file.txt` -> Print starting from line 20 to the end.

These are less common but powerful for scripting.
